mechanism_explained: Wavelength sets the resolution ceiling (Abbe ~200nm light; ~0.004nm electrons); resolution trades inversely against field of view across the EM-to-PET ladder.
CT measures X-ray attenuation (whole-body, sub-mm, mSv dose); ultrasound measures reflected sound (local, real-time); both localize masses but cannot diagnose cancer.
MRI images proton relaxation (T1/T2); quantitative MRI (ADC, DCE) measures cellularity and vascularity, detecting response before size change; standardization unsettled.
PET reconstructs a radiotracer location from positron annihilation/coincidence detection, mapping function at coarse 4-6mm resolution; a proxy for activity, not malignancy.
One ligand (PSMA), two isotopes: image with Ga-68/F-18 PET, treat with Lu-177; the scan predicts where the drug can deliver dose, lesion by lesion.
A fluorophore absorbs one color and emits a longer one (Stokes shift); how you reject unwanted photons (pinhole, two-photon, super-res) sets the scale and question.
Hydrodynamically focused cells cross a laser one at a time; scatter and per-cell fluorescence give population fractions, built by gating+compensation, not read directly.
An antibody deposits stain on a specific protein in intact tissue; companion-diagnostic scores (HER2 0-3+, PD-L1 TPS%) are thresholded interpretations of a continuous signal.
EM trades the finest resolution (TEM ~0.1nm, SEM ~1-5nm, AFM sub-nm) for a killed/fixed/vacuum specimen; artifact-vs-biology, settled by cross-checking preps, is the dominant problem.
Every preclinical modality measures a depth- and delivery-dependent proxy, so an efficacy claim requires concordance across burden/anatomy/function, never a single image.
Imaging is intervention infrastructure: per-fraction accuracy (CBCT, MR-Linac, gating) reduces the geometric margin, which permits high, conformal, organ-sparing dose.
Multimodal integration adds information only when sources fail independently and cover each others scale blind spots; fusion is not averaging, and AI/radiomics fusion is unsettled.
